title: Linear Flows on Translation Prisms
speaker: Jayadev Athreya
abstract: Motivated by the study of billiards in polyhedra, we study linear flows in a family of singular flat 3-manifolds which we call translation prisms. Using ideas of Furstenberg, and Veech, we connect results about weak mixing properties of flows on translation surfaces to ergodic properties of linear flows on translation prisms, and use this to obtain several results about unique ergodicity of these prism flows and related billiard flows. Furthermore, we construct explicit eigenfunctions for translation flows in pseudo-Anosov directions with Pisot expansion factors, and use this construction to build explicit examples of non-ergodic prism flows, and non-ergodic billiard flows in a right prism over a regular n-gon for n=7,9,14,16,18,20,24,30. This is joint work with Nicolas Bedaride, Pat Hooper, and Pascal Hubert.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Rational-prism billiard setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates the problem with eigenfunction images, defines polygonal billiards, restricts to rational polygons where unfolding gives finite translation-surface dynamics, and introduces rational prisms as the three-dimensional systems to study.

proof_stages.label: Ergodic and spectral vocabulary
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls ergodicity, Birkhoff time averages, eigenfunctions, and weak mixing, emphasizing that weak mixing is equivalent to the absence of nontrivial eigenfunctions and to a Cesaro mixing condition.

proof_stages.label: Furstenberg Fourier criterion for circle extensions
proof_stages.summary: For a product or skew-product flow over a base system with an S^1 fiber moving at constant speed, Fourier expansion in the fiber shows that invariant functions exist exactly when the fiber speed resonates with an eigenvalue of the base flow.

proof_stages.label: Almost-every equidistribution in rational prisms
proof_stages.summary: The first main theorem states and proves that for any rational prism, almost every direction gives equidistribution for every well-defined orbit, because the resonant vertical speeds form a measure-zero generalized spectrum.

proof_stages.label: Translation-surface renormalization and pseudo-Anosov recurrence
proof_stages.summary: The talk places unfolded billiards in the broader setting of translation surfaces, describes recent weak-mixing results, defines the GL(2,R) action and Teichmuller geodesic renormalization, and singles out periodic renormalization as pseudo-Anosov dynamics.

proof_stages.label: Pisot pseudo-Anosov torus factor
proof_stages.summary: If a pseudo-Anosov expansion factor is a Pisot number of degree d, a cohomological construction produces a continuous surjection from the translation surface to a d-torus that semiconjugates the vertical flow to a linear flow in the expanding eigendirection of an integral matrix.

proof_stages.label: Eigenfunctions and explicit bad prism directions
proof_stages.summary: The torus-factor coordinates are eigenfunctions, and in the double-heptagon/regular-heptagon prism case they yield explicit non-equidistributing directions with nonzero vertical speed and fractal-looking invariant level sets visualized in the opening pictures.
